label: Formula 25.2b
section: DETERMINISTIC SINGLE-LOCUS THEORY
subsection: Dudley's Estimators of a, n, and $ p_{0} $
nearby_text: Dudley (1977) noted that, in a divergence selection experiment (Chapter 18), Equation 25.2a yields the limit, $ R_{H} $, for response in the high direction, while $$ \begin{align*}{R_L\over\sigma_A}={2nap_0\over\sqrt{2na^2p_0(1-p_0)}}=\sqrt{{2np_0\over1-p_0}}\end{align*} $$ (25.2b) yields the limit to response in the low direction. Taking the ratio of these two limits yields $$ \frac{R_{H}}{R_{L}}=\frac{\sqrt{2n(1-p_{0})/p_{0}}}{\sqrt{2np_{0}/(1-p_{0})}}=\frac{1-p_{0}}{p_{0}} $

}

label: Formula 25.8d
section: MAJOR GENES VERSUS POLYGENIC RESPONSE: THEORY
subsection: Lande's Model: Response With a Major Gene in an Infinitesimal Background
nearby_text: n ( $ \mu_{s_i} $), the second term is the mean for i before selection ( $ \mu_i $), and $ S_i = \mu_{s_i} - \mu_i $. From Equations 25.7b and 25.8b, the expected change in the polygenic mean becomes $$ \Delta\mu=h^{2}\left[(1-p)^{2}\frac{\overline{W}_{0}}{\overline{W}}S_{0}+2p(1-p)(1-s_{1})\frac{\overline{W}_{1}}{\overline{W}}S_{1}+p^{2}(1-s_{2})\frac{\overline{W}_{2}}{\overline{W}}S_{2}\right] $$ (25.8d) From [[SEE_TABLE:25.2]], the new mean becomes $$ \overline{z}=(\mu+\Delta\mu)\Biggl[1+2\alpha_{1}(p+\Delta p)(1-p-\Delta p)+\alpha_{2}\left(p+\Delta p\right)^{2}\Biggr] $$ (25.8e)

label: Formula 25.9a
section: MAJOR GENES VERSUS POLYGENIC RESPONSE: THEORY
subsection: Lande's Model: Response With a Major Gene in an Infinitesimal Background
nearby_text: $ w(z) \simeq 1 + \beta z $, for weak selection ( $ |\beta z| \ll 1 $). Lande showed that under exponential fitnesses, his model is nicely behaved, with the polygenic mean evolving at a constant rate $$ \Delta\mu=\sigma_{A}^{2}\beta $$ (25.9a) while the relationships between the major-locus genotypic fitnesses remain constant, with $$ \frac{\overline{W}_{i}}{\overline{W}_{0}}=e^{\beta\alpha_{i}}\quad for i=1,2 $$ (25.9b)

  "nearby_text": "$ w(z) \\simeq 1 + \\beta z $, for weak selection ( $ |\\beta z| \\ll 1 $). Lande showed that under exponential fitnesses, his model is nicely behaved, with the polygenic mean evolving at a constant rate $$ \\Delta\\mu=\\sigma_{A}^{2}\\beta $$ (25.9a) while the relationships between the major-locus genotypic fitnesses remain constant, with $$ \\frac{\\overline{W}_{i}}{\\overline{W}_{0}}=e^{\\beta\\alpha_{i}}\\quad for i=1,2 $$ (25.9b)"
}

label: Formula 25.10b
section: AN OVERVIEW OF LONG-TERM SELECTION EXPERIMENTS

nearby_text: This motivated Herrendörfer and Bünger (1988) and Bünger and Herrendörfer (1994) to suggest using an exponential regression of the form $$ R_{c}(t)=a\left[1-\exp\left(-bt/a\right)\right] $$ (25.10b) where a is the selection limit and b is the maximum rate of response (i.e., the response in the first generation). label: Formula 25.10d
section: AN OVERVIEW OF LONG-TERM SELECTION EXPERIMENTS
subsection: Estimating Selection Limits and Half-lives
nearby_text: r and Bünger 1988). Assuming that $ R_{c}(t) $ is well approximated by an exponential regression, this is given by the derivative of Equation 25.10b, evaluated at the generation of interest, yielding $$ \frac{\partial R_{c}(t)}{\partial t}=b\cdot\exp(-b t/a) $$ (25.10d) As was the case for linear regressions of short-term response, one decision is whether to regress cumulative response on number of generations or on the cumulative selection differential (Chapter 18)

section: AN OVERVIEW OF LONG-TERM SELECTION EXPERIMENTS

nearby_text: A much more intriguing function for very long-term selection data is a power curve, where $$ R_{c}(t)=b t^{a}\quad for a<1 $$ (25.11b) which has the feature that while the rate of response decelerates over time ( $ \partial^2 \mu(t) / \partial t^2 < 0 $ for $ a < 1 $), there is no upper limit. The power curve provided a better fit t

label: Formula 25.12b
section: CONFLICTS BETWEEN NATURAL AND ARTIFICIAL SELECTION
subsection: Accumulation of Lethals in Selected Lines
nearby_text: Following the loss of lethal homozygotes in the surviving offspring, $ \hat{p} $ decreases to $$ \widetilde{p}=\frac{(1/2)\mathrm{freq}(Bb)}{\mathrm{freq}(Bb)+\mathrm{freq}(bb)}=\frac{\widehat{p}(1-\widehat{p})}{2\widehat{p}(1-\widehat{p})+(1-\widehat{p})^{2}}=\frac{\widehat{p}}{1+\widehat{p}} $$ (25.12b)

label: Formula 25.13
section: CONFLICTS BETWEEN NATURAL AND ARTIFICIAL SELECTION
subsection: Accumulation of Lethals in Selected Lines
nearby_text: r(z_{Bb}>T)+\left(\frac{1-p}{1+p}\right)\Pr(z_{bb}>T) $$ Because $ (z_{Bb} - \mu - a)/\sigma \sim U $ and $ (z_{bb} - \mu)/\sigma \sim U $, where $ U $ denotes a unit normal, this rearranges to yield $$ q(1+p)=2p\Pr\left(U>T^{*}-\frac{a}{\sigma}\right)+(1-p)\Pr(U>T^{*}) $$ (25.13) where $ T^{*} = (T - \mu)/\sigma $. The frequency of Bb following artificial selection becomes $$ \mathrm{freq}^{\prime\prime}(Bb)=\left(\frac{2p}{1+p}\right)\frac{\Pr(U>T^{*}-a/\sigma)}{q} $$ yieldi

nearby_text: Likewise, the equilibrium frequency, $ \tilde{p} $, following the removal of lethals (BB homozygotes) is $$ \widetilde{p}=\frac{(1/2)\mathrm{freq}(Bb)}{1-\mathrm{freq}(BB)}=\frac{\widehat{p}\left(1-\widehat{p}\right)}{1-\widehat{p}^{2}}=\frac{\widehat{p}}{1+\widehat{p}} $$ (25.15b)

section: CONFLICTS BETWEEN NATURAL AND ARTIFICIAL SELECTION
subsection: Lerner's Model of Genetic Homeostasis
nearby_text: atural selection ( $ \bar{\nu}a/\sigma_z > s_1 $), $ B $ is fixed. section: CONFLICTS BETWEEN NATURAL AND ARTIFICIAL SELECTION
subsection: Artificial Selection Countered by Natural Stabilizing Selection
nearby_text: Now suppose directional selection operates with a selection intensity of $ \bar{\imath} $, yielding a selection differential from artificial selection of $$ S_{a}=\bar{\imath}\sigma_{z^{*}}=\frac{\bar{\imath}\sigma_{z}\omega}{\sqrt{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}} $$ (25.17c) for a total selection differential of $$ S=S_{a}+S_{s t}=\frac{\overline{\imath}\sigma_{z}\omega}{\sqrt{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}}-\mu\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}} $$ (25.17d)

        "label": "Formula 25.8b",

        "label": "Formula 25.8a",

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\frac{\\partial W_{i}}{\\partial\\mu}&=\\int w(z)\\frac{\\partial\\varphi(z,\\mu+\\alpha_{i},\\sigma^{2})}{\\partial\\mu}dz\\\\&=\\frac{1}{\\sigma^{2}}\\left[\\int z w(z)\\varphi(z,\\mu+\\alpha_{i},\\sigma^{2})dz-(\\mu+\\alpha_{i})\\int w(z)\\varphi(z,\\mu+\\alpha_{i},\\sigma^{2})dz\\right]\\\\&=\\frac{1}{\\sigma^{2}}\\left[\\int z w(z)p_{i}(z)dz-(\\mu+\\alpha_{i})\\ \\overline{W}_{i}\\right]=\\frac{\\overline{W}_{i}}{\\sigma^{2}}\\ S_{i}\\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula 25.8b",
        "section": "MAJOR GENES VERSUS POLYGENIC RESPONSE: THEORY",

        "latex": "\\widehat{p}=\\frac{s_{2}+\\bar{\\imath}\\left(a/\\sigma_{z}\\right)\\left(1-s_{2}\\right)}{s_{1}+s_{2}+\\bar{\\imath}\\left(a/\\sigma_{z}\\right)\\left(s_{1}-s_{2}\\right)}\\simeq\\frac{s_{2}+\\bar{\\imath}\\left(a/\\sigma_{z}\\right)}{s_{1}+s_{2}}\\quad for\\quad s_{1},s_{2}\\ll0",
